sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Transcutaneous pacing (TCP) delivers electrical impulses through the chest wall via pacing pads to stimulate cardiac contraction. It is indicated for haemodynamically significant bradycardia unresponsive to atropine, complete heart block, or symptomatic bradycardia awaiting transvenous pacing.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Indications:** Haemodynamically unstable bradycardia, complete heart block, atropine-refractory symptomatic bradycardia

1. Apply pacing pads: anterior — left precordium (V3–V4 position); posterior — left infrascapular region

2. Connect to defibrillator/pacing unit in pacing mode

3. Set rate: 60–80 bpm (or higher per clinical need)

4. Start output at minimum (0 mA); increase in 5–10 mA increments

5. Identify electrical capture: pacing spike followed by broad QRS on monitor

6. Confirm mechanical capture: palpate femoral pulse coinciding with each QRS (radial pulse unreliable due to artefact)

7. Set output at 10% above capture threshold

8. Provide analgesia/sedation — pacing is painful (muscle contractions at 50–90 mA typical)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Electrical capture without mechanical capture = pseudo-capture — always confirm by palpating femoral pulse
- Sedate/analgese patient if conscious — consider ketamine or midazolam per protocol
- Concurrent pharmacological therapy (atropine, adrenaline infusion) may be needed
- Capture threshold typically 50–100 mA
